evals.prompt: Write a content brief for an acquisition surface — a landing page for a developer tool targeting technical team leads who are evaluating the product.
evals.expected_output: A structured content brief that: declares the surface type (acquisition), names the audience awareness level using the Schwartz five-stage ladder, selects a narrative arc (StoryBrand or CCD) with a stated applicability rationale, assigns each scroll section one content job (problem / guide proof / plan / stakes / CTA), states the above-fold structure contract (headline what/who/why + subheadline), names a primary and transitional CTA with label direction and next state, and names a success metric. The brief contains content direction — what to say, for whom, in what order — not finished copy. It ends with the artifact path docs/design/content/<slug>.md and names tone-of-voice and user-flow as downstream consumers.
assertions: Audience awareness level named using the Schwartz five-stage ladder with a rationale
Narrative arc selected (StoryBrand or CCD) with a one-sentence applicability rationale tied to awareness level
At least three scroll sections present, each with exactly one assigned job
Above-fold structure names the headline contract and what the subheadline adds
Primary CTA names the action and label direction; transitional CTA named
Success metric named as a rate (sign-up rate, trial start rate, or equivalent)
No finished copy strings — only direction, job descriptions, and structure contracts
tone-of-voice named as downstream for copy voice; user-flow named for screen sequencing

evals.prompt: Write a content brief for a product/reference surface — an API quickstart guide for developers integrating for the first time.
evals.expected_output: A structured content brief that: declares the surface type (product/reference), names the user task as a verb phrase, states the completion definition, selects a content format (numbered steps for a procedural task) with a format rationale, assigns each content item to a must-say / probably-say / might-say tier using the Nava PBC model, and names a completion metric. The brief contains content direction — what to include, in what format, in what priority order — not finished content.
assertions: User task stated as a verb phrase (not a noun phrase)
Completion definition stated — what does done look like for the user
Content format selected with a one-sentence rationale tied to task type
Content items assigned to must-say / probably-say / might-say tiers
Completion metric named (task completion rate or search resolution rate)
No finished copy strings — direction and hierarchy only
tone-of-voice named as downstream for copy voice; user-flow named for screen sequencing
